title: Observable — Why Salt Is a Crystal
logline: Sodium gives an electron, chlorine takes it — the resulting ions lock into a repeating NaCl rock-salt lattice from crystallography data.

brand.title: Observable
brand.subtitle: Upon Tyne Science — evidence before wonder
brand.cta: Follow Observable — one measurement at a time.

science_subject.key_measurement: Lattice parameter a ≈ 5.64 Å (NaCl, COD 1000040); 6:6 coordination
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific crystal lattice visualization, rock-salt NaCl structure, alternating Na+ and Cl- ions on face-centered cubic grid, 6:6 coordination, ball-and-stick with unit cell outline, neutral dark background, accurate relative ion positions, NOT TO SCALE if unit cell repeated, 16:9 documentary fidelity

beats.speech_text: Table salt looks like a simple white grain — but inside, every speck is a perfectly repeating grid of charged atoms locked by electrostatic glue.
beats.on_screen: Observable · Why Salt Is a Crystal

beats.speech_text: Sodium has one electron to lose; chlorine wants one to gain. Transfer makes Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions. Opposites attract — but in a solid, each ion is tugged by neighbors in all directions, settling into a rock-salt lattice: alternating sodium and chlorine on a cubic grid.

X-ray diffraction maps where atoms sit in a crystal. For sodium chloride, the spacing and the six-neighbor geometry are catalogued in open crystallography databases — COD entry one-zero-zero-zero-zero-four-zero — not inferred from a textbook sketch.
This model shows the repeating unit: purple chloride, gold sodium, each ion touched by six partners. We repeat the cell for clarity — a teaching model, not a microscope photograph of one grain.
beats.visualization_prompt: NaCl rock-salt crystal lattice, alternating Na+ and Cl- ions, face-centered cubic unit cell, 6:6 coordination, ball-and-stick, chemistry crystallography fidelity

beats.speech_text: Ionic crystals are everywhere — table salt, ceramics, battery electrolytes. The same electrostatic accounting that builds salt also sets melting points, hardness, and why shattering a crystal cleaves along flat planes.

lines: EVIDENCE: COD 1000040 crystallography; ionic solid textbook consensus
VIZ: Structural MODEL from open crystallography — NOT TO SCALE
